headline: Kenji described a confidence dip driven by late changing priorities, with engagement and clarity both landing at a low point.
summary_bullets: Kenji repeatedly links uncertainty to late direction changes: “changing direction late” and “priorities change fast” make him feel like he’s “guessing.”
He asks for support in a specific form—“a regular calibration loop”—but also worries about help: “I worry about sounding slow.”
His “good” is operational, not emotional: “short weekly calibration on what good looks like for my current tickets.”
understanding_paragraph: Kenji is not struggling with effort; he’s struggling with decision stability. The session clarified that when priorities shift late, he loses confidence in what to optimize for and feels unsure what “good” looks like week to week. He wants more ownership, but only alongside clearer guardrails and frequent, specific calibration.

axes.meaning: Frustration shows up as shakiness and confidence dips when direction changes late; worth watching over the next few weeks.
Engagement is at a low point because he alternates between feeling “useful” and feeling “like I am guessing.”
Clarity is weak: he’s “still unsure what good really looks like… week to week,” especially during fast priority shifts.
Growth is constrained by missing guardrails; he wants ownership plus “clearer guardrails” to learn with confidence.

brutal_truth_employee: Kenji’s core signal is that late changes break his confidence: “When priorities change fast, I lose confidence in what to optimize for.” He also self-censors support—“I worry about sounding slow”—even though he’s asking for a “regular calibration loop.”
brutal_truth_manager: When Kenji offered a concrete mechanism—“A regular calibration loop would make this feel less shaky”—the conversation stayed on tracks that could have pulled even more specifics (what cadence, who attends, and what artifacts define “good”). The moment to deepen was after “I worry about sounding slow,” where an exploratory follow-up on what “slow” means in his mind would have unlocked the exact barrier to asking for help.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 20-minute weekly calibration with Kenji for his current tickets and confirm the definition of “good” he wants to use.
Create a lightweight guardrails doc for Kenji’s work (what can change, what cannot, and how late changes get communicated) and share it with him.
Ask Kenji to pick one calibration input he wants most (priorities, acceptance criteria, or feedback timing) and commit to it for the next two weeks.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Kenji stops mentioning “guessing” and instead references a stable “good” definition, the calibration loop is working.
Before next 1:1: if Kenji still hesitates to ask for help after specific, close-to-work feedback, the “sounding slow” barrier remains.
